title: W4 D Sugggested Exercises
body:
Suggested Exercises for Week 4 Juan Carlos Carbajal

ECON 3202 — Term 1, 2026 UNSW Sydney

Problem 1. Show whether or not the following sequences converge. ! "∞ (a) x k = 2k +1 3k+4 . k =1 # $∞ (b) yk = k12 k=1 . ! "∞ (c) zk = k+k 1 . k =1

Problem 2. Prove the following proposition.

Proposition 1

Consider two sequences { x k }∞ k ∞ k k=1 and { y }k =1 in R, and suppose that x → x and yk → y. Then:

(a) x k + yk → x + y.

(b) α x k → α x, for all α ∈ R.

(c) x k yk → x y.

(d) If yk ∕= 0 for all k and y ∕= 0, then x k /yk → x/y.

We have seen parts (a) and (b) during the lectures. Part (d) is a bit tricky so feel free to leave it aside on your first attempt.

Problem 3. Let f be a real-valued function defined on R by

f ( x ) = x2 + x + 1.

(a) Show that for any sequence of real numbers { x k }∞ k=1 converging to 1, one has

lim f ( x k ) = 3. k→∞

(b) Does this suffice to establish that f is continuous at x̂ = 1?

(c) Argue whether or not f is continuous everywhere. (d) Expand your argument to show that any polynomial P : R → R of degree m defined by

P ( x ) = α0 + α1 x + α2 x 2 + + α m x m

is continuous.

Problem 4. Prove the following proposition.

Proposition 2

Let f and g be two real-valued functions defined on R. If f and g are continuous at x ∈ R, then

(a) f + g is continuous at x.

(b) α f is continuous at x.

(c) f × g is continuous at x.

Problem 5. This serves as a cautionary tale. Most of the time it is convenient to think of continuity of a function in terms of sequences because it works better with our intuition. Yet sometimes our intuition is not right. Consider the following function f : R2 → R: % & x2 1 x2 2 , if ( x1 , x2 ) ∕= (0, 0); x1 + x2 f ( x1 , x2 ) = '0 if ( x1 , x2 ) = (0, 0).

Using convergent sequences, show that f is discontinuous at (0, 0).

Problem 6. The following is the standard formulation of the utility maximization problem (UMP) in classic demand theory. There are n goods in the economy. A bundle of goods is x = ( x1 , , xn ), where xi ≥ 0 represents the amount of good i in the bundle x, for all i = 1, , n. Each good i is sold in a competitive market at price pi > 0. A consumer has preferences represented by a continuous utility function u : R n+ → R. The consumer is endowed with wealth w > 0 and maximizes her utility subject to a budget constraint: n maxn u( x) x ∈R + subject to ∑ pi xi ≤ w. i =1

(a) Write down the budget set for the consumer. Notice this is the feasible set for the UMP. Argue as best as you can that the budget set is closed and bounded. (b) Show that the UMP has an optimal solution.

(c) What can go wrong when pi = 0 for some i = 1, , n? (d) Go back to the case where all prices are strictly positive. Suppose that good 1 represents a ‘necessity’ (like your first morning coffee). Given the current inflation situation, the government has imposed a limit on the amount of good 1 that can be bought. Each consumer can spend at most half her wealth on good 1. Argue whether or not the UMP has an optimal solution in this case.

Problem 7. A typical situation studied in the theory of choice under uncertainty can be described as follows. There are n prizes to be won, each of which is valued by the decision maker according to the utility vector

v = ( v1 , , v n ) ∈ R n .

Here the real number vi represents the utility the decision maker derives from winning prize i = 1, , n. A lottery is a probability distribution over said prizes. I will represent a lottery by the Greek letter µ, i.e., µ = ( µ1 , , µ n ).

Each µi represents the probability of winning prize i if the decision maker buys the lottery µ. As such, it must be that n 0 ≤ µi ≤ 1, for all i = 1, , n, and ∑ µi = 1. i =1

Let L denote the set of all possible lotteries. The decision maker chooses a lottery to maximize her expected utility: n max U (µ) = ∑ µi vi subject to µ ∈ L. µ i =1

Argue that the decision maker’s problem has a solution. That is, there exists a lottery µ∗ ∈ L that maximizes her expected utility.
